question: Is the DHRS design adequate for removing decay heat following a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater?

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, specifying the types of steel and alloys for each component, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting, all chosen for corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. It also references relevant material specifications from the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) to ensure compliance.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, ensuring safe cooling without reliance on external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours of an initiating event, preventing fuel or pressure boundary limits from being exceeded. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) and meets regulatory design and reliability requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems in the NuScale Power Module, detailing the integration of the reactor, steam generators, pressurizer, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). It also outlines the configuration and boundaries of the steam generator system, emphasizing the integral design of the steam generators within the RPV and the separation between primary and secondary sides.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details how actuation valves are tested during cold shutdown conditions, outlines the procedures for valve exercising and position verification, and specifies that a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function will be performed on the first installed reactor module as part of the initial test program.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for degasification and emergency core cooling to maintain safety and integrity. It interfaces with several other systems, including chemical control, emergency cooling, and control rod drives.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operation and Maintenance (OM) Code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC-endorsed standards, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as tables summarizing relevant ASME code cases for reactor components.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters for both primary and secondary systems, considering criteria such as maximizing electrical output, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining safety margins. Key parameters like coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures are calculated using thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand, with uncertainties addressed through testing and design requirements. The design supports natural circulation with minimal pressure losses, and uncertainties related to steam generator tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient cooling. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper water chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to prevent fuel design limit violations during all operational conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

edges.description: Section A describes the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is part of the reactor coolant system that interfaces with the DHRS described in Section B, providing structural and material context for the system whose adequacy in decay heat removal is being evaluated.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system components, including the DHRS, while Section B specifies detailed material information for the pressurizer, which is one of the components described in Section A.

Section A identifies the DHRS as a subsystem within the reactor coolant system, while Section B details the DHRS's design basis and its function to remove decay heat following loss of normal feedwater, directly addressing the analysis question.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which supports Section B's claim that the DHRS is designed to reliably remove decay heat following a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor coolant system's degasification and pressure boundary functions, while Section B specifies the detailed materials and components of the pressurizer that are referenced in Section A's system description.

Section B specifies that the DHRS is designed to remove decay heat and ensure safe shutdown without external power when normal feedwater is unavailable, while Section A describes RCS features (like vent valves) that support emergency cooling operations, indicating that the RCS design elements in A support the DHRS function described in B.
Section B references Section A by citing Section 5.2 for material descriptions of the pressurizer components, establishing a direct cross-reference between the two sections.

Section B describes the design basis and function of the DHRS in removing decay heat after a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater, while Section A discusses overpressure protection and code compliance for the reactor coolant boundary, which is a prerequisite for the DHRS to function as intended.

Section A describes the inclusion and design of the DHRS within the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, connecting them through the regulatory prerequisites for DHRS adequacy.

Section A lists the DHRS as a component included in the reactor coolant system design, while Section B provides a summary description of the RCS and its functions, indicating that the DHRS is part of the overall system described in B.

Section A describes the inclusion of the DHRS as a component within the reactor coolant system design, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that the system, including DHRS, must comply with for integrity and overpressure protection, thus connecting the design features in A to the regulatory requirements in B.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, ensuring their reliability, while Section B describes the RCS features (such as venting noncondensible gases) that support DHRS operation during emergencies, connecting the adequacy of DHRS design to the supporting RCS functions.

Section A states that DHRS components, including actuation valves, meet preservice and inservice inspection requirements per Section XI of the BPVC, while Section B discusses compliance with ASME BPVC and related code cases for design and construction, indicating that both sections address adherence to ASME BPVC standards for safety-related components.

Section A describes system features (such as degasification and venting) that contribute to maintaining the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that these design features must comply with.

Section B describes the pressurizer materials and references Section 5.2, which is cited in Section A, thereby connecting the detailed component design in B to the general material integrity and standards outlined in A.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the quality assurance and material reliability requirements, which are foundational to the design described in Section B for the DHRS's ability to remove decay heat following a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater.

Section A provides references to industry standards and guidelines that are cited in Section B to support the detailed design description of the Reactor Coolant System and its subsystems, including the DHRS.
Section A references Section B by directing the reader to Section 5.4 for further description of the steam generators, which are relevant to the DHRS's ability to remove decay heat.

Section A describes the system evaluation criteria and mentions the DHRS's connection to the steam generator for decay heat removal, while Section B specifies the DHRS's design basis to remove decay heat and achieve safe shutdown when normal cooling is unavailable, thus Section A's evaluation contextually supports and is detailed by Section B's design basis.

Section A discusses the system evaluation and performance criteria for decay heat removal using the steam generators (SGs) and DHRS, while Section B provides the detailed design and components of the reactor coolant system, including the DHRS and SGs, thus Section B specifies the technical details for the general system evaluation described in Section A.

Section A provides references to standards and guidelines that underpin the quality assurance and material reliability requirements, which support the system evaluation and performance criteria described in Section B for the DHRS's ability to remove decay heat.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system, which includes the pressurizer described in detail in Section B, thus Section B specifies the materials and components referenced in Section A's summary.

Section A describes the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation, which is the operational basis that enables the DHRS described in Section B to remove decay heat following a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B specifies the detailed components and subsystems of the RCS, including the decay heat removal system (DHRS) relevant to the analysis question.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system design, while Section B specifies detailed material and component information for the pressurizer, which is a subsystem described in Section A.

Section A describes the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation, which is the operational basis that enables the DHRS in Section B to remove decay heat following a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater.
Section B provides detailed descriptions of the RCS components, including the DHRS, that are generally introduced in Section A's summary of the system design.

Section A and Section B are identical text excerpts, both providing a summary description of the reactor coolant system (RCS) design basis, thus they are structurally referencing the same information.
